coredata.dc:title: Manganese oxide doped carbon dots for temperature-responsive biosensing and target bioimaging
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chunmei
Qin, Zhaojian
Wang, Maonan
Liu, Weiwei
Jiang, Hui
Wang, Xuemei
coredata.dc:description: We report on the synthesis of manganese oxide doped CDs (MnOx-CDs) by a hydrothermal strategy using manganese (III) acetylacetonate (Mn(III) (C5H7O2)3) as the only raw materials. The MnOx-CDs exhibit water solubility, favorable biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and show blue fluorescence with excitation/emission maxima at 326/442 nm with a quantum yield of 11.3%, allowing efficient cellular imaging. The MnOx-CDs have a reversible temperature-sensitive fluorescent property in vitro within 10–60 °C, which can also be used as a sensitive thermometer in living cells. By a scratch assay, the MnOx-CDs can restrain the migration of HepG2 cancer cells, which make the MnOx-CDs be attractive candidates for liver cancer adjuvant treatment. Besides, the fluorescence of the MnOx-CDs is quenched in the presence of Fe3+ due to the formation of a nonfluorescent MnOx-CDs-Fe3+ complex between oxygen-containing groups on the surface of MnOx-CDs and Fe3+, and the quenched fluorescence of MnOx-CDs can be turn-on by dissociation of MnOx-CDs-Fe3+ complexes by biothiols including L-cysteine, homocysteine and glutathione. Therefore, the Fe3+ and biothiols can be sequentially detected with high reliability and accuracy via exploiting the on-off-on nanosensor at room temperature, respectively. Further application to detection biothiols in human serum indicated that the probe was practicality and feasibility in medical field.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Manganese oxide-doped carbon dots
Fluorescence probe
Cellular imaging
Thermometer

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fluorimetric detection of Fe3+ and L-cys at room temperature
Temperature-responsive behaviors in vitro and its biological application in live cell
Determining for Fe3+ in vitro at room temperature based on MnOx-CDs
Determination of L-cys in vitro at room temperature based on the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China
National Key Research and Development Program of China Stem Cell and Translational Research

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) UV–vis absorption (a), excitation (b, λem = 442 nm) and emission (c, λex = 350 nm) spectra of the MnOx-CDs, both excitation and emission slit width were 5 nm; (B) XPS spectrum of the MnOx-CDs; High resolution C1s (C), O1s (D) and Mn2p (E) XPS spectra of the MnOx-CDs; (F) TEM image of the MnOx-CDs, inset: a size distribution histogram of the MnOx-CDs.

(A) FL/FL0-temperature plots of MnOx-CDs during cooling and heating processes; (B) The cycle experiment of the temperature response over MnOx-CDs; Confocal laser scanning microscopy images (excited at 405 nm) of MnOx-CDs in HepG2 cells at 40 °C (C), at 30 °C (D) and at 20 °C (E); (F) Fluorescent microscope image to evaluate wound healing in vitro by the scratch assay using a HepG2 cells. Herein, FL0 is the fluorescent intensity of the MnOx-CDs at 10 °C and FL are the fluorescent intensities of the MnOx-CDs at various temperature.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of the MnOx-CDs, the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system and the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system in the presence of L-cys, wherein the concentrations of Fe3+ and L-cys are 10 μM and 3 μM, respectively; (B) Apparent zeta potential of the MnOx-CDs, the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system and the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system in the presence of L-cys; (C) Fluorescence emission spectra of the MnOx-CDs in the presence of different concentrations of Fe3+ (0–50 μM); (D) The relationships between (FL0-FLi)/FL0 and Fe3+ ions (0–50 μM), the inset is the linear plot, FLi and FL0 are the FL intensities at 442 nm of MnOx-CDs in the presence and absence of Fe3+, respectively; (E) Fluorescence emission spectra of the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system upon addition of various concentrations of L-cys; (F) The relationships between (FLq-FLi)/FLi and L-cys, FLi and FLq are the FL intensities at 442 nm of MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system in the absence and presence of L-cys, respectively. The concentration of MnOx-CDs used in the above is 20 μg/mL.
Schematic diagram of the synthesis of MnOx-CDs and the mechanism of detection of Fe3+ and biothiols by the MnOx-CDs.

head.ce:title: Manganese oxide doped carbon dots for temperature-responsive biosensing and target bioimaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Biomedical Engineering Education, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 210096, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: National Demonstration Center for Experimental Biomedical Engineering Education
School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Bioelectronics, National Demonstration Center for Experimental Biomedical Engineering Education, School of Biological Science and Medical Engineering, Southeast University, Nanjing, 210096, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report on the synthesis of manganese oxide doped CDs (MnOx-CDs) by a hydrothermal strategy using manganese (III) acetylacetonate (Mn(III) (C5H7O2)3) as the only raw materials. The MnOx-CDs exhibit water solubility, favorable biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity, and show blue fluorescence with excitation/emission maxima at 326/442 nm with a quantum yield of 11.3%, allowing efficient cellular imaging. The MnOx-CDs have a reversible temperature-sensitive fluorescent property in vitro within 10–60 °C, which can also be used as a sensitive thermometer in living cells. By a scratch assay, the MnOx-CDs can restrain the migration of HepG2 cancer cells, which make the MnOx-CDs be attractive candidates for liver cancer adjuvant treatment. Besides, the fluorescence of the MnOx-CDs is quenched in the presence of Fe3+ due to the formation of a nonfluorescent MnOx-CDs-Fe3+ complex between oxygen-containing groups on the surface of MnOx-CDs and Fe3+, and the quenched fluorescence of MnOx-CDs can be turn-on by dissociation of MnOx-CDs-Fe3+ complexes by biothiols including L-cysteine, homocysteine and glutathione. Therefore, the Fe3+ and biothiols can be sequentially detected with high reliability and accuracy via exploiting the on-off-on nanosensor at room temperature, respectively. Further application to detection biothiols in human serum indicated that the probe was practicality and feasibility in medical field.

ce:section.ce:para: Biothiols are thiol-containing compounds that play important roles in a plethora of biological processes, and commonly include L-cysteine (L-cys), homocysteine (Hcy) and glutathione (GSH) [1,2]. They have been identified in biological fluids and exert protective effects on some human diseases [3]. In order to achieve physiological function mentioned above of the biothiols, the level of the biothiols in human body must be within an appropriate level, otherwise intracellular metabolic disorders even organ/body damage may occur. Thus, it is essential to fabricate effective methods to detect and image biothiols in human tissues, human body fluids, and cells, especially in tumor cells which is of high significance to establish early diagnosis and treatment of tumor disease.

With the application of nanomaterials in analytical research, a variety of nanomaterials, such as nanocomposites [4,5], nanoclusters [6], nanofibers [7,8], have been applied to detect biothiols. As one of emerging nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) have attracted intensive interest and have also been used to determine biothiols due to their fascinating photophysical properties, small size, biocompatibility and chemical inertness [9]. Doped CDs are considered the most useful CDs and have been actively used as biothiols probe [10–12]. However, doping heteroatoms in CDs used to construct biothiols probe are usually nonmetallic elements, such as nitrogen, phosphorus, sulfur, selenium, silicon, boron, halogen, nitrogen and sulfur, and nitrogen and phosphorus [10–14]. Intuitively, biothiols probe based on metal elements or metal oxides doped CDs should produce unexpected properties in the analytical field due to their electronic characteristics and fascinating photophysical properties of metal elements or metal oxides doped CDs. Hence, a new pathway is of paramount importance to synthesize biothiols probe based on metal elements or metal oxides doped CDs.

Herein, we set up a new biothiols probe based on manganese oxide doped CDs (MnOx-CDs). The MnOx-CDs are synthesized for the first time by a hydrothermal strategy using manganese (III) acetylacetonate (Mn(III) (C5H7O2)3) as the only raw materials. The MnOx-CDs are water solubility, favorable biocompatibility, low cytotoxicity and show blue fluorescence with excitation/emission maxima at 326/442 nm, because of which they can be used for cellular imaging. The MnOx-CDs have a reversible temperature-sensitive fluorescent property in vitro within 10–60 °C and can be used as a thermometer in living cells. By a scratch assay, the MnOx-CDs can restrain the migration of HepG2 cells, which make the MnOx-CDs be attractive candidates for liver cancer adjuvant treatment. Besides, the fluorescence of the MnOx-CDs is obviously turn-off by forming a nonfluorescent MnOx-CDs-Fe3+ complex, and the quenched fluorescence can be turn-on by separation of MnOx-CDs-Fe3+ complex to form a more stable metal-thiol complex (Fe3+-biothiol complex) shown in Scheme 1 . Therefore, successive, selective and sensitive detection of both Fe3+ ions and biothiols at room temperature is achieved.

Manganese (III) acetylacetonate (Mn(III) (C5H7O2)3) was purchased from Aladdin Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China, www.aladdin-e.com). Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate and L-cysteine (L-cys) were purchased from Sinoreagent Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China, www.sinoreagent.com). Homocysteine (Hcy), glutathione (GSH), ascorbic acid (AA) were purchased from Sigama-Aldrich (USA, www.sigmaaldrich.com). Metal (e.g., Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Au3+, Fe2+ and Ni2+) salts were purchased from Sinoreagent Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. (Shanghai, China, www.sinoreagent.com), and other metal (e.g., Ag+ and Pb2+) salts were purchased from Mandel Scientific Company Inc. (Guelph, ON, Canada, www.mandel.ca). All remaining chemical substances and solvents are of analytical grade and do not need to be pre-purification when using. Ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ cm, Millipore, USA) was used throughout.

The fluorescence and UV–vis absorption spectra were acquired by using an RF-5301PC fluorometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and a Biomate 3S spectrophotometer (Thermo Fisher, USA), respectively. The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were measured on an Axis UltraDLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) were carried out on a JEM-2100 microscope operating at 200 kV (JEOL, Japan). The FT-IR spectroscopy was performed on a Nicolet iS5 IR spectrometer (Thermo Fisher, USA) in the range of 400–4000 cm−1. The fluorescent lifetimes were measured based on a PluoroLog 3-TCSPC transient steady-state fluorescence spectrophotometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon, France) with the excitation wavelength at 360 nm. The energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis was performed by an Ultra Plus scanning electron microscope (Zeiss, Germany). The zeta-potentials were measured by using a Malvern Zetasizer Nano ZS (Malvern Instr., UK).
The synthetic details of MnOx-CDs were illustrated in the Electronic Supporting Materials (ESM), and the purified MnOx-CDs were preserved at room temperature, with a concentration of 4 mg mL−1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorimetric detection of Fe3+ and L-cys at room temperature
ce:section.ce:para: A fluorescent procedure for Fe3+ and L-cys was conducted according to typical methods. Briefly, 200 μL phosphate buffered saline (PBS) (10 mM, pH = 7.4) which contains 5% NaCl to warrant physiological ionic strength, 10 μL purified MnOx-CDs and different amounts of Fe3+ were mixed. Then, the obtained mixtures were diluted to 2 mL with ultrapure water. Thirty minutes later, their fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorometer at room temperature with excitation at 350 nm.

For analyzing L-cys, PBS (10 mM, pH = 7.4) of 200 μL, purified MnOx-CDs of 10 μL, ultrapure water of 500 μL and Fe3+ (50 μM) of 200 μL were successively added into each of a series of 2 mL centrifuge tubes. Five minutes later, different amounts of L-cys were placed in the above centrifuge tubes, respectively, and then diluted to 2 mL with ultrapure water and evenly mixed. Thirty minutes later, their fluorescence spectra were recorded by a fluorometer at room temperature with excitation at 350 nm.

The cytotoxicity of MnOx-CDs was studied according to a 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyl tetrazolium bromide (MTT) assay. Briefly, 1000–10,000 cells were seeded in RPMI 1640 media containing fetal bovine serum (FBS, 10%) on a 96-well plate and were incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, MnOx-CDs with different amounts were added into the cells. After 12 h incubation, 20 μL MTT (5 mg mL−1) solution was added into each cell well. After 4 h incubation, cell culture solution was removed from each cell and then 150 μL dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) was added into each cell. The absorbance of MTT at 570 nm of each well was measured with a microplate reader within 10 min after the addition of DMSO.

For cell imaging experiments, HepG2 cells were firstly seeded in a confocal dish (ϕ15 mm) for 6 h in cell culture medium. Then, MnOx-CDs (50 μg mL−1) were added into the dish. After incubation for 4 h, the cell culture solution containing MnOx-CDs was removed and the cells were washed with PBS three times. Fluorescence images of cells were obtained on a Ti-E + C2 laser scanning confocal microscope (Nikon, Japan).

HepG2 cancer cells (about 50,000/well) were plated in RPMI 1640 media (containing 2% FBS) in the absence or presence of MnOx-CDs (50 μg mL−1) in 6-well plates, and were incubated at 37 °C with 5% CO2 overnight. Following, the scratches were made in the monolayers using the pinpoint of a pipette tip, and the RPMI 1640 media were removed from the 6-well plates. Then, the HepG2 cancer cells in the 6-well plate were rinsed gently with 10 mM PBS for several times and then were incubated in cell culture medium. Images of cells were captured on a microscope (Nikon, Japan) at 0 h, 6 h and 12 h after scratch treatment, respectively.

The wound healing percentage was determined by the area change of scratch area, which is expressed as follow:
%
W o u n d
h e a l i n g
p e r c e n t a g e

S c r a t c h
a r e a
( 0 )
− S c r a t c h

( i )

× 100
where
S c r a t c h
a r e a
( 0 )
and
S c r a t c h
a r e a
( i )
represent the scratch area at 0 h and
i
h after scratch treatment, respectively.

Metal acetylacetonates are β-diketonate coordination complexes derived from the acetylacetonate anion and metal ions, especially transition metal ions, and have been used to synthesize metal nanoparticles [15]. Mn(III) acetylacetonate (Mn(III) (C5H7O2)3), as one of the metal acetylacetonates, has a six-membered chelate ring formed by both oxygen atoms binding to the Mn (III) center [16] and has already been used to prepare metal nanoparticle [17,18]. Due to its excellent intrinsic quality, for the first time Mn(III) (C5H7O2)3 serving as the only raw material was used to synthesize the MnOx-CDs.

The as-prepared MnOx-CDs show excellent water solubility and have two typical UV–vis absorption peaks (Fig. 1 A, curve a), i.e., a peak at 241 nm which is ascribed to π-π* transition of CC, and a peak at 270 nm corresponding to n-π* transition of the CO bond [19,20]. The MnOx-CDs show a blue fluorescence with a maximum fluorescent excitation peak centered at 326 nm (Fig. 1A, curve b) and a maximum fluorescent emission peak centered at 442 nm (Fig. 1A, curve c). As shown in Fig. S1 in the Electronic Supporting Materials (ESM), the MnOx-CDs exhibit excitation-dependent emission when changing the excitation wavelengths from 320 nm to 370 nm. Using quinine sulfate in 0.1 M H2SO4 (QY: 0.542) as a standard, the QY of MnOx-CDs is 11.3%. Furthermore, MnOx-CDs can also be stable at high salt concentrations, which can be deduced from the little decrease of the fluorescence intensity even when the concentration of NaCl reaches 100 mM (Fig. S2A in ESM). Fig. S2B (in ESM) shows the fluorescence intensities of the MnOx-CDs are almost constant in a wide pH range of pH 3.77–9.51. It should be noted that the emission intensity of MnOx-CDs does not show significant change even after exposure to UV light for 4800 s (Fig. S2C in ESM), suggesting the prominent anti-photobleaching capability. Owing to the excellent stability of photoluminescence, MnOx-CDs are a good candidate for use as an effective fluorescent probe under complicated environments.

The XPS spectrum of MnOx-CDs indicate the presence of C, O and Mn element. As shown in the full range XPS spectrum of the MnOx-CDs (Fig. 1B), the MnOx-CDs have three peaks at 284.6, 531.6 and 642.3 eV which are attributed to C1s (67.92 wt%), O1s (29.19 wt%) and Mn2p (2.89 wt%), respectively. The C1s zone (Fig. 1C) can be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.8 eV (graphitic sp2 carbon structure), 287.5 eV (C–OH) and 288.9 eV (O–CO) [21,22]. The deconvolution of O1s zone (Fig. 1D) indicates the presence of Mn–O–C bonding according to the peak at 530.9 eV [23]. The peak at 531.7 eV of O1s spectrum represents the presence of some manganese oxides connected with O–CO [23]. The remaining two peaks of 532.0 and 532.5 eV in the O1s spectrum are ascribed to C–OH and C–O–C groups [24], respectively. As shown in Fig. 1E, the high resolution Mn2p spectrum of the MnOx-CDs reveals that the Mn2p3/2 is spin-orbit doublet with two peaks at 641.9 eV and 644.8 eV, which correspond to Mn3+ and Mn4+ oxidation states, respectively [23]. These XPS results of the MnOx-CDs suggest the formation of MnOx (i.e., MnO2 and Mn2O3) in the doped CDs.

The morphology and size of the MnOx-CDs were characterized by TEM. As illustrated in Fig. 1F, the MnOx-CDs are well dispersed spherical particles with uniform and smaller size distribution (approximate 5.65 ± 0.30 nm calculated by measuring 150 particles). The HRTEM image of MnOx-CDs (Fig. S3 in ESM) reveals the presence of crystalline structures in MnOx-CDs with lattice planes (0.23 nm spacing) which corresponds to crystal planes of MnOx in the MnOx-CDs [25,26]. The TEM and HRTEM results are also consistent with the above XPS results. The FT-IR spectra of MnOx-CDs and Mn (III) acetylacetonate exhibit three differences, including peak at 2477 cm−1, region A and region B (Fig. S4 in ESM). These differences prove the successful synthesis of MnOx-CDs. Generally, the FT-IR spectrum of MnOx-CDs confirms the presence of C–OH stretching vibrations at 3600-3200 cm−1, –CO stretching vibrations at 1549 cm−1 and -C-O-C- stretching vibrations at 1396 cm−1 in the exterior of MnOx-CDs [27].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Temperature-responsive behaviors in vitro and its biological application in live cell
ce:section.ce:para: The MnOx-CDs also exhibit interestingly temperature-sensitive fluorescent properties. The fluorescent intensity of MnOx-CDs decreases as the temperature increases which is ascribed to the increased probability of non-radiative transition of the MnOx-CDs when the temperature rises. At low temperatures, the radiative transition of MnOx-CDs plays a dominant role, therefore the number of emitted photons increases with the decreasing temperatures [28]. As shown in Fig. 2 A, during the cooling process, there is excellent linear relationship between the FL/FL0 and temperature in the range 60–10 °C, with a calibration equation as FL/FL0 = 1.0445 × C - 0.0051 (R2 = 0.997). Similarly, during the heating process, FL/FL0 is also proportional to temperature in the range 10–60 °C, with a calibration equation as FL/FL0 = 1.0664 × C - 0.0066 (R2 = 0.998) (Fig. 2A). Herein, FL0 is the fluorescent intensity of the MnOx-CDs at 10 °C and FL are the fluorescent intensities of the MnOx-CDs at various temperature. Therefore, the MnOx-CDs can be used as a reversible thermometer for practical applications. Fig. 2B shows the reversible fluorescent response to temperature during seven runs of temperature switch between 20 °C and 60 °C, which indicates that MnOx-CDs can be a candidate as a reversible thermometer.

To explore the temperature-responsive fluorescent properties of MnOx-CDs in biological environments, we have investigated the HepG2 cells incubated with MnOx-CDs at different culture temperatures. Firstly, the cytotoxicity of MnOx-CDs was studied by MTT assays in HepG2 and L02 cells. As shown in Fig. S5 in ESM, the MnOx-CDs are biocompatibility and low toxicity based on a fact that a high cell viability (77.7% after incubation for 24 h) is still observed after treatment with MnOx-CDs at 0.64 mg mL−1 [29]. As illustrated by confocal laser scanning microscopy (excited at 405 nm), the blue luminescence of the MnOx-CDs in the HepG2 cells is weak when the environmental temperature was 40 °C (Fig. 2C), and the blue luminescence of the MnOx-CDs in HepG2 cells enhances as the temperature decreased to 30 °C (Fig. 2D) even to 20 °C (Fig. 2E). Fig. S6 shows HepG2 cells themselves has no blue luminescence without incubation by MnOx-CDs at 20 °C, 30 °C and 40 °C. Due to the temperature-responsive properties, the as-synthesized MnOx-CDs can be readily applied in the biomedical fields like bioimaging and photothermal therapy in cancer treatment.

We have also explored the effect of MnOx-CDs on the migration of HepG2 cells to the wounded area by a scratch assay, wherein the scratch assay is a comparatively conventional method for assessing cells (especially fibroblast) migration to and proliferation into the would area [30]. As shown in Fig. 2F, the migration of HepG2 cells in the absence and presence of MnOx-CDs were studied after 0, 6 and 12 h (Fig. 2F). By comparing wound healing percentage of the Control group (29.2%) and the MnOx-CDs group (16.6%), the MnOx-CDs can restrain the migration of HepG2 cells.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determining for Fe3+ in vitro at room temperature based on MnOx-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the feasibility for metal ions probe based on MnOx-CDs, the fluorescent intensity of MnOx-CDs in the presence of certain metal ions (e.g., Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Au3+, Fe2+, Fe3+, Ni2+, Ag+ and Pb2+) were measured. Fig. S7A and Fig. S7B in ESM displays the fluorescent intensity of the MnOx-CDs was remarkably quenched when introducing Fe3+ and Pb2+, while the remaining metal ions showed no significant fluorescent inhibition effect. Some previous literature [10,31] reported that Hg2+ also can quench fluorescence of CDs. In order to eliminate interference from Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions, PBS containing high concentrations of SO4 2− (5%) was used to remove Hg2+ and Pb2+ ions in advance and the fluorescence of the MnOx-CDs in PBS containing high concentrations of SO4 2− (5%) was steady. The presence of reduction molecules, including GSH, L-cys, Hcy and AA cannot cause significant changes in the fluorescence intensity of MnOx-CDs. Therefore, the MnOx-CDs own very high stability and selectivity, which make MnOx-CDs be attractive candidates for sensing Fe3+.

The fluorescent quenching phenomenon caused by Fe3+ is also shown in Fig. 3 A. According to previous literatures [32–35], the fluorescent quenching phenomenon caused by Fe3+ can be ascribed to the binding of Fe3+ to the surface of MnOx-CDs through the specific interaction between surface oxygen-containing groups of MnOx-CDs and Fe3+. Subsequently, a nonfluorescent complex (i.e., MnOx-CDs-Fe3+ complex) was formed by the binding of Fe3+ to the surface of the MnOx-CDs, which was proved based on a designed experiment and UV–vis absorption spectra in Fig. S10. The details of the designed experiment and related data were described in Fig. S8 and Fig. S9. To further explore the quenching effect of Fe3+ on the MnOx-CDs, we also measured the zeta potential of MnOx-CDs and the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system. As shown in Fig. 3B, the zeta potential of MnOx-CDs changes from −19.8 mV to 32.2 mV upon addition of Fe3+. This satisfies the hypothesis that the fluorescent quenching of the MnOx-CDs was caused by the formation of the nonfluorescent complex.

Furthermore, the fluorescence lifetime of MnOx-CDs and the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system are measured (Fig. S11). The quenching effect of Fe3+ on the MnOx-CDs obeys a simple static quenching mechanism based on the fact that there is no obvious change in average fluorescence lifetime of the MnOx-CDs with the addition of Fe3+ (2.68 ns) comparing with MnOx-CDs alone (2.95 ns) (see Table S1 in ESM).

To optimize the experimental conditions, it is clear that the fluorescence intensity at 442 nm is gradually decreased upon the reaction with different concentrations (0–50 μM) of Fe3+ (Fig. 3C). The plot of the ratio of quenched fluorescence ((FL0-FLi)/FL0) at 442 nm against the concentration of Fe3+ are is shown in Fig. 3D. The inset in Fig. 3D reveals there is an excellent linear correlation between the (FL0-FLi)/FL0 and the concentrations of Fe3+ in the concentration range 0.02–0.6 μM, with a calibration curve expressed as (FL0-FLi)/FL0 = 1.119 × c + 0.0745 (c: μM, R2 = 0.998). The limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ is 16 nM (LOD = 3σ/s, wherein σ is the standard deviation of three blank samples and s is the slope of the calibration curve), which is lower than LOD values detected by previous literatures [32,34,36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of L-cys in vitro at room temperature based on the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system
ce:section.ce:para: In the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system discussed above, Fe3+ ions are stably linked to the surface of the MnOx-CDs. However, upon adding L-cys to the system, Fe3+ may detach from the MnOx-CDs due to a metal-thiol bond formed by the Fe3+ ions and L-cys which is stronger than the specific interaction between the Fe3+ ions and MnOx-CDs. As shown in Fig. 3B, the zeta potential of the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system also changes from 32.2 to 28.8 mV after adding negatively charged L-cys. Meanwhile, the fluorescent intensity recovers after introducing L-cys to the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system (curve blue and red in Fig. 3A). As shown in Fig. 3E, the fluorescence intensity at 442 nm is gradually increased upon the reaction with different concentrations (0–3 μM) of L-cys. The plot of the fluorescent recovery ratio ((FLq-FLi)/FLi) at 442 nm against the concentration of L-cys is shown in Fig. 3F, and there is an excellent linear correlation between the (FLq-FLi)/FLi and the concentrations of L-cys in the concentration range of 0.05–0.6 μM. The calibration curve is expressed as (FLq-FLi)/FLi = 4.830 × c + 0.618 (c: μM, R2 = 0.996) and the LOD for L-cys is 23 nM (LOD = 3σ/s). Table S2 (ESM) shows the comparison of detection performances for L-cys by the current MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system with other nanomaterials-based methods reported in previous literature, which reveals that the LOD of the L-cys probe developed by our method is relatively low. The average fluorescence lifetime of the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system (2.68 ns) and MnOx-CDs/Fe3+/L-cys system (2.88 ns) calculated by the results of fluorescence lifetime spectra in Fig. S11 are also coincident, demonstrating that the fluorescent quenching of the MnOx-CDs is attributed to a simple static quenching mechanism and is caused by the formation of the nonfluorescent complex.

It has been demonstrated that the sensitivity influence of thiol-containing compounds, such as Hcy and GSH on L-cys is significant, while sensitivity influence of other thiol-free amino acids is scarcely [31]. Therefore, we also evaluate the selectivity influence of thiol-containing compounds on the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system to L-cys (in Fig. S12 in ESM). It is clear that Hcy and GSH also result in a significant fluorescence enhancement of the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system. Besides, Hcy and GSH also had the linear range of 0.15–2 μM and 0.2–2 μM, respectively, and their detection limit was 29 and 34 nM, respectively (Fig. S13 in ESM). This clearly suggests that the fluorescence enhancement of the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system is actually attributed to the formation metal-thiol bond between Fe3+ and biothiols.

To further evaluate the feasibility and reliability of this probe based on MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system in biological samples, the MnOx-CDs/Fe3+ system was further studied as a probe to detect biothiols in FBS diluted by PBS (10 mM). For quantitative measurement, a standard addition method with L-cys as the standard was used to analyze the concentrations of biothiols in the samples. The method shows good recovery rates (Table S3 in ESI), which suggests the feasibility and reliability of the present method for biothiols determination in practical applications.

In summary, we have synthesized MnOx-CDs by one-pot hydrothermal treatment using Mn (III) acetylacetonate as the single precursor. It has proved that the form of MnO2 and Mn2O3 are coexisted in MnOx-CDs. The MnOx-CDs show excellent fluorescent properties in vitro and with fluorescence excitation/emission maxima at 326/442 nm. Oxygen-containing groups (e.g., abundant carboxyl and hydroxyl) on the surface of the MnOx-CDs allows the specific binding of Fe3+, resulting in the formation of non-fluorescent MnOx-CDs-Fe3+ complexes. Based on the interaction between MnOx-CDs and Fe3+, a fluorescence probe for Fe3+ with a linear range of 0.02–0.6 μM and a LOD of 16 nM was demonstrated. Moreover, the quenched fluorescence can also be recovered after the dissociation of MnOx-CDs-Fe3+ complex by biothiols. Therefore, successive, selective and sensitive detection of both Fe3+ and biothiols have been achieved. Besides, the MnOx-CDs have a reversible temperature-sensitive fluorescent property in vitro within 10–60 °C and can also be used as a thermometer in living cells. The MnOx-CDs can restrain the migration of HepG2 cancer cells, which make the MnOx-CDs be attractive candidates for liver cancer adjuvant treatment. This raises the possibility to further explore the role of manganese oxide doped CDs in efficient liver cancer adjuvant treatment in future biomedical applications.

Chunmei Li: Conceptualization, Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Methodology, Project administration, Resources, Software, Visualization, Writing - original draft, Writing - review & editing. Zhaojian Qin: Methodology, Software. Maonan Wang: Methodology, Software. Weiwei Liu: Software, Visualization. Hui Jiang: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Resources, Writing - review & editing. Xuemei Wang: Resources, Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing - review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Key Research and Development Program of China (2017YFA0205300), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21675023, 91753106, and 81325011) and Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019716).
